stories_covered: Nvidia data centers in space concept
harry_takes: Travis Kalanick would have a trillion-dollar Uber today if it existed five years in the future—a counterfactual on how AI changes business value
Nvidia stock didn't move on trillion-dollar news because it was already priced into analyst forecasts; the real story is four-five years of 30-50% capex growth is being assumed
Jason is abandoning small-TAM seed investing because excess capital destroys returns and power law dynamics dominate—he only bets on massive addressable markets now
Layoffs from Atlassian and Meta aren't about efficiency; they're admissions that managers don't need half their teams because AI handles the work
harry_boldest_quote: The bigger your fund size, the more you have to be a power law junkie.
predictions: Lemkin: Half of tech employees are unnecessary for current roles; with AI, enterprises will cut by at least 20%, some by 40%
Lemkin: $50-100M seed funds will be worst-returning funds of this vintage due to power-law valuations on non-unicorn outcomes
Both: Era of stair-step TAM expansion is over; if you can't make a case for multi-billion TAM, you can't raise at seed today
disagreements: Harry questions whether massive TAMs must exist pre-launch (Replit was a niche pre-AI, Figma Make is design, Decagon is support); Jason says those exceptions don't change the rule—you need hundred-billion-dollar outcomes to justify today's seed prices.
harry_role: Questioning moderator challenging Jason's new determinism. Pushes back on power-law myopia. Points out small TAMs have birthed giants. Plays steward of the traditional seed playbook.
notable: Jason explicitly renounces the classic VC playbook of betting small with great founders; he can no longer take meetings with non-billion-TAM companies, signaling a structural shift in early-stage investing.
